questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: In projectile motion, the time of flight depends only on the vertical component of initial velocity and acceleration due to gravity.
Statement II: The horizontal component of velocity has no effect on the time of flight.

options.a: Both statements are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
options.b: Both statements are true but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is true, Statement II is false

questions.explanation:
Resolve the launch velocity into components: $u_x = u\cos\theta$, $u_y = u\sin\theta$.
Vertical motion obeys $y(t) = u_y t - \tfrac{1}{2} g t^2$. For a projectile launched and landing at the same level, put $y=0$ and solve for $t$ to get the non-trivial root $T = \tfrac{2u_y}{g} = \tfrac{2u\sin\theta}{g}$.
Notice $u_x$ does not appear in this expression—horizontal motion is uniform (no horizontal acceleration in ideal conditions), so it only affects the range, not $T$.
Assumptions: same launch/landing height, constant $g$, and negligible air resistance. Under these standard assumptions, Statement II directly explains Statement I.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass $2\,\text{kg}$ rests on a rough horizontal surface with coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.2$. A horizontal force of $3\,\text{N}$ is applied. Will the block move? (Take $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
questions.explanation:
Normal reaction: $N = mg = 2\times10 = 20\,\text{N}$.
Maximum static friction: $f_{\max} = \mu N = 0.2\times20 = 4\,\text{N}$.
Applied force $F=3\,\text{N}$ is less than $f_{\max}$, so static friction can fully balance it. The block stays at rest and the actual static friction equals $3\,\text{N}$ (opposing the applied force).
Acceleration $=0$ since net force is zero. It would begin to move only if $F>f_{\max}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Work done by centripetal force in uniform circular motion is zero.
Reason (R): Centripetal force is always perpendicular to the displacement of the particle at every instant.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation:
Instantaneous displacement $d\vec{r}$ is tangential, while centripetal force $\vec{F}_c$ points toward the center (radially inward). Thus $\vec{F}_c \perp d\vec{r}$ at each instant.
Work element: $dW = \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r} = F\,dr\,\cos90^\circ = 0$. Hence the total work $W = \int dW = 0$.
By the work–energy theorem, zero work implies kinetic energy (and speed) remains constant, consistent with uniform circular motion. Therefore R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two wires of the same material and length are stretched under the same tension. One wire has double the radius of the other. The ratio of their fundamental frequencies is:
questions.explanation:
For a stretched string fixed at both ends, the fundamental frequency is $f = \tfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\tfrac{T}{\mu}}$, where $\mu$ is the linear mass density.
With same material and length, $\mu = \rho A = \rho\pi r^2$ so $\mu \propto r^2$. If one wire has radius $2r$, then its $\mu$ becomes $4\mu$ of the thin wire.
Thus $f \propto 1/\sqrt{\mu}$, so the thicker wire has half the frequency of the thinner wire. Therefore $f_{\text{thin}} : f_{\text{thick}} = 2:1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about $\text{sun\text{-}synchronous}$ (near-polar) satellites is correct?

options.a: They hover over one longitude like geostationary satellites
options.b: They typically have an orbital period of $\,\approx 24\,\text{h}$
options.c: They pass over a given point at nearly the $\text{same local solar time}$ each day, ideal for imaging

questions.explanation:
Sun-synchronous orbits are near-polar (inclination $\sim 98^\circ$), with periods $\sim\!100\,\text{min}$ at a few hundred km altitude. Due to Earth’s oblateness, the orbital plane precesses at a rate that keeps the local solar time of the ground track nearly constant.
This constant lighting geometry is excellent for remote sensing and Earth observation. They do not hover (that’s geostationary), are not equatorial, and their period is far less than $24\,\text{h}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sound source emits waves of frequency $500\,\text{Hz}$. An observer moves towards the source with speed $20\,\text{m/s}$. If the speed of sound in air is $340\,\text{m/s}$, what frequency does the observer hear?
questions.explanation:
For a moving observer and stationary source, Doppler formula is $f' = f\,\tfrac{v + v_o}{v}$, where $v$ is sound speed and $v_o$ the observer speed towards the source.
Substitute: $f' = 500\,\tfrac{340+20}{340} = 500\times\tfrac{360}{340} \approx 529\,\text{Hz}$.
The increase occurs because successive wavefronts are encountered more frequently as the observer moves towards them (air is at rest; no wind).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In a transformer, the core is laminated.
Reason (R): Laminations reduce eddy current losses by increasing the effective resistance of eddy current paths.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation:
Alternating flux in the core induces circulating eddy currents (by Faraday’s law). These currents cause $I^2R$ losses and heating.
Laminating the core into thin, insulated sheets reduces the cross-section available for eddy loops and dramatically increases the effective resistance of those loops, hence reducing the eddy current magnitude and loss. (Hysteresis loss is minimized by choosing suitable core material; lamination specifically targets eddy currents.)
Thus R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A concave mirror of focal length $20\,\text{cm}$ forms an image of an object placed $30\,\text{cm}$ in front of it. What is the nature and position of the image?

options.c: Real, inverted, formed at $60\,\text{cm}$ behind the mirror

questions.explanation:
Mirror formula: $\tfrac{1}{f} = \tfrac{1}{v} + \tfrac{1}{u}$. Using the Cartesian sign convention for mirrors: take $f=-20\,\text{cm}$ (concave), $u=-30\,\text{cm}$ (object in front).
$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{-20} - \dfrac{1}{-30} = -\tfrac{1}{20} + \tfrac{1}{30} = -\tfrac{1}{60} \Rightarrow v = -60\,\text{cm}$.
Negative $v$ means the image is on the same side as the object (in front of the mirror), hence real and inverted. Position $60\,\text{cm}$ from the mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two capacitors $4\,\mu\text{F}$ and $6\,\mu\text{F}$ are connected in series across a $12\,\text{V}$ battery. What is the potential difference across the $6\,\mu\text{F}$ capacitor?
questions.explanation:
Series combination: $\dfrac{1}{C_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{5}{12} \Rightarrow C_{eq} = 2.4\,\mu\text{F}$.
Total charge: $Q = C_{eq}V = 2.4\times12 = 28.8\,\mu\text{C}$. In series, each capacitor has the same $Q$.
Voltage across $6\,\mu\text{F}$: $V_6 = Q/C_6 = 28.8/6 = 4.8\,\text{V}$. (Check: $V_4 = 28.8/4=7.2\,\text{V}$ and $V_4+V_6=12\,\text{V}$.)
Hence the $6\,\mu\text{F}$ capacitor has $4.8\,\text{V}$ across it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Current through an ideal inductor lags behind voltage by $90^{\circ}$ in an AC circuit.
Reason (R): In an inductor, $V = L\,\tfrac{di}{dt}$, so voltage leads current by $90^{\circ}$.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation:
Let the current be sinusoidal: $i(t) = I_m\sin\omega t$. Then $\dfrac{di}{dt} = I_m\omega\cos\omega t = I_m\omega\sin\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$.
From $v(t)=L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$, the inductor voltage is a cosine (or a sine shifted by $+90^{\circ}$), i.e. it reaches its peaks a quarter cycle before the current does. Hence the voltage leads the current by $90^{\circ}$, or equivalently, the current lags the voltage by $90^{\circ}$.
This phase relation is the defining reactive behavior of an ideal inductor (average power over a cycle is zero).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: The escape velocity from Earth does not depend on the mass of the object.
Statement II: Escape velocity is derived from the condition that the total mechanical energy of the body at infinity is zero.

options.a: Both statements I and II are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
options.b: Both statements I and II are true but II does not explain I
options.c: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
options.d: Statement I is false but Statement II is true

questions.explanation:
Set the total mechanical energy to zero at infinity (marginal escape): $E_\infty = K_\infty + U_\infty = 0 + 0 = 0$. At Earth's surface, $E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}mv_e^2 - \dfrac{GMm}{R}$. Conservation $E_0 = E_\infty$ gives $\tfrac{1}{2}mv_e^2 - \dfrac{GMm}{R} = 0 \Rightarrow v_e = \sqrt{\tfrac{2GM}{R}}$.
The object's mass $m$ cancels, so $v_e$ depends only on Earth's $M$ and $R$. Statement II (derivation condition) directly explains Statement I (mass-independence).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is dropped from a height of $20\,\text{m}$. What is the velocity with which it strikes the ground? (Take $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation:
Using kinematics for free fall: $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$. With $u=0$, $g=10\,\text{m/s}^2$, $h=20\,\text{m}$: $v = \sqrt{2\times10\times20} = \sqrt{400} = 20\,\text{m/s}$.
(Equivalently by energy: $mgh = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{2gh}$.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A convex lens can produce both real and virtual images.
Reason (R): The nature of the image depends on the position of the object with respect to the focal length of the lens.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Lens equation: $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u}$. For a convex lens, $f>0$. If the object is placed beyond the focal length ($|u|>f$), solving gives $v>0$ (real image on the opposite side), which is inverted. If the object is within the focal length ($0<|u|Thus the image nature depends on $u$ relative to $f$, explaining how a convex lens can form both real and virtual images.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of $2\,\text{A}$ flows through a conductor for $5\,\text{s}$. How many electrons pass through the conductor in this time? (Charge of electron $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$)
questions.explanation:
Total charge transported: $Q = It = 2\times5 = 10\,\text{C}$. Number of electrons $n = Q/e = \dfrac{10}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = 6.25\times10^{19}$.
So $6.25\times10^{19}$ electrons pass the cross-section in $5\,\text{s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A body moving with uniform speed in a straight line has zero acceleration.
Reason (R): Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, and if both magnitude and direction are constant, acceleration is zero.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation:
Velocity is a vector. Uniform rectilinear motion means constant speed and unchanging direction, so $\vec{v}$ is constant. Acceleration is $\vec{a} = d\vec{v}/dt = 0$.
(Contrast: in uniform circular motion speed is constant but direction changes, so $\vec{a}\neq 0$.) Reason correctly explains the assertion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistors of $6\,\Omega$ and $3\,\Omega$ are connected in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance?
questions.explanation:
Parallel rule: $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{6} + \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{1}{6} + \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Therefore $R_{eq} = 2\,\Omega$.
The equivalent of parallel resistors is less than the smallest branch resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: The magnetic field at the center of a circular current-carrying loop is directly proportional to the current.
Statement II: It is inversely proportional to the radius of the loop.
questions.explanation:
From the Biot–Savart law for a loop of radius $R$: $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$ at the center (for one turn). Thus $B\propto I$ and $B\propto 1/R$.
(For $N$ turns: $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$, same proportionalities hold.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The half-life of a radioactive substance is 10 days. After 30 days, what fraction of the original sample will remain?
questions.explanation:
Number of half-lives elapsed: $n = \dfrac{30}{10} = 3$. Remaining fraction after $n$ half-lives: $(1/2)^n = (1/2)^3 = 1/8$.
So $12.5\%$ of the original activity (or nuclei) remains.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A diverging lens always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image of an object placed anywhere.
Reason (R): Diverging lenses cause parallel rays to diverge as if coming from a principal focus on the same side.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation:
A diverging (concave) lens has negative focal length. For any real object, the refracted rays diverge and appear to originate from a point between the lens and its focal point on the object side—giving a virtual, erect, diminished image.
Ray rule: a ray initially parallel to the axis emerges as if from the near focus, encapsulated by the statement in R, which explains why the image is always virtual and smaller.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electric bulb rated $60\,\text{W}, 220\,\text{V}$ is used for 1 hour. How much energy is consumed?
questions.explanation:
Energy $E = Pt$. With $P = 60\,\text{W} = 60\,\text{J/s}$ and $t = 1\,\text{h} = 3600\,\text{s}$: $E = 60\times3600 = 216000\,\text{J} = 2.16\times10^5\,\text{J}$.
(In practical units: $60\,\text{W}\times1\,\text{h} = 60\,\text{Wh} = 0.06\,\text{kWh}$.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): A geostationary satellite appears stationary to an observer on Earth.
Reason (R): Its orbital period is equal to the rotational period of Earth and it revolves in the equatorial plane from west to east.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: A satellite will appear fixed over one longitude only if its angular velocity matches Earth's rotation and its orbit lies in the equatorial plane with prograde motion (west→east). For a circular geostationary orbit, $T \approx 24\,\text{h}$ (one sidereal day) and inclination $=0^{\circ}$. With the same angular speed and equatorial plane, the sub-satellite point stays constant, so the satellite appears stationary. Hence A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical springs, each of spring constant $k$, are connected in parallel. What is the effective spring constant of the system?
questions.explanation: For parallel springs under the same extension $x$, individual forces add: $F=F_1+F_2=kx+kx=2kx$. By definition $F=k_{eq}x$, so $k_{eq}=2k$. (Contrast: in series, extensions add for the same force, giving $k_{eq}=\left(\tfrac{1}{k}+\tfrac{1}{k}\right)^{-1}=k/2$.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The output of a logic gate is $1$ only when **both** inputs are $1$. Which gate is this?
questions.explanation: The AND gate implements the logical product: $Y=A\cdot B$. Its truth table yields $Y=1$ only for $(A,B)=(1,1)$. OR gives $1$ if either input is $1$, NAND is the complement of AND, and NOR is the complement of OR.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an RC circuit, a capacitor of capacitance $C$ is charged through a resistor of resistance $R$ by a DC source of emf $V$. The time at which the capacitor voltage first reaches $(1 - e^{-1})V$ is:
questions.explanation: Charging follows $v_C(t)=V\big(1-e^{-t/RC}\big)$. Set $v_C=(1-e^{-1})V$ to obtain $1-e^{-t/RC}=1-e^{-1}\Rightarrow e^{-t/RC}=e^{-1}\Rightarrow t=RC=\tau$. The result is independent of $V$; it depends only on the time constant $\tau$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with amplitude $5\,\text{cm}$ and time period $2\,\text{s}$. What is the maximum velocity of the particle?
questions.explanation: For SHM, $v_{max}=\omega A$ with $\omega=2\pi/T$. Given $T=2\,\text{s}$, $\omega=\pi\,\text{rad/s}$. With $A=5\,\text{cm}$, $v_{max}=\pi\times5=5\pi$ cm/s. Velocity is maximum at the mean position where $x=0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In Young’s double slit experiment, fringe width increases if the distance between slits and screen is increased.
Reason (R): Fringe width is directly proportional to the distance between the screen and slits.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Fringe width in YDSE is $\beta=\dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$, where $D$ is the slit–screen distance and $d$ is slit separation. Thus $\beta\propto D$ (for fixed $\lambda$ and $d$). Increasing $D$ increases spacing between adjacent bright (or dark) fringes. Therefore A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The emf induced in a coil of $N$ turns, when the magnetic flux through each turn changes at a rate $d\Phi/dt$, is:
questions.explanation: Faraday’s law states $\varepsilon=-N\,\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. The magnitude is $|\varepsilon|=N\,\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. The negative sign (Lenz’s law) indicates that the induced emf opposes the change in flux that produces it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two bodies of masses $2\,\text{kg}$ and $4\,\text{kg}$ are moving with velocities $2\,\text{m/s}$ and $1\,\text{m/s}$ respectively in the same direction. They collide perfectly inelastically. What is their common velocity after collision?
questions.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, bodies stick together and momentum is conserved: $v=\dfrac{m_1u_1+m_2u_2}{m_1+m_2}=\dfrac{2\cdot2+4\cdot1}{2+4}=\dfrac{8}{6}=1.33\,\text{m/s}$. Kinetic energy is not conserved, but total momentum is.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The pressure at the bottom of a liquid column depends on the density of the liquid and height of the column.
Reason (R): Pressure at depth is given by $P = h\rho g$.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: From hydrostatic equilibrium of a fluid element: $dP=\rho g\,dh \Rightarrow P(h)=\rho gh$ (taking atmospheric pressure as reference). Thus pressure at depth $h$ depends on $\rho$ and $h$ (and $g$), independent of container shape. Hence R provides the governing formula that explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of resistance $10\,\Omega$ is stretched to double its length. What is its new resistance?
questions.explanation: With resistivity $\rho$ constant and volume conserved, $R=\rho\dfrac{L}{A}$. Doubling the length to $2L$ halves the cross-sectional area to $A/2$ (since $AL=\text{const}$). Thus $R' = \rho\dfrac{2L}{A/2}=4\rho\dfrac{L}{A}=4R=40\,\Omega$. Resistance quadruples when length doubles at constant volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, the net external torque about that axis equals the time rate of change of angular momentum about the same axis.
Reason (R): Angular momentum of a rigid body about a fixed axis is $I\omega$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $\omega$ is angular velocity.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Rotational dynamics is governed by Newton’s second law in angular form: $\tau_{net} = \dfrac{dL}{dt}$. For a rigid body with constant moment of inertia about a fixed axis, angular momentum is $L = I\omega$. Differentiating: $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = I\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is angular acceleration. Thus torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum, and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A gas expands quasi-statically from state A to B along a straight line on a P–V diagram (pressure decreases linearly with volume). Which statement is correct about the work done by the gas?

options.a: W is equal to the area under the line AB and is positive for expansion
options.b: W is independent of the path between A and B
options.d: W equals zero because internal energy is a state function

questions.explanation: Work done in a quasi-static process is given by $W = \int P\,dV$, which is the geometric area under the P–V curve. For expansion ($\Delta V > 0$), the integral is positive. Work is path-dependent, unlike internal energy, which is a state function. Thus the correct statement is that $W$ equals the area under AB and is positive for expansion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin prism of small refracting angle $A$ has refractive index $\mu$. For small $A$, which relation holds at minimum deviation?
questions.explanation: For a prism, refractive index is $\mu = \dfrac{\sin[(A+\delta_{min})/2]}{\sin(A/2)}$. For a thin prism (small $A$), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). This simplifies to $\mu \approx 1 + \dfrac{\delta_{min}}{A}$, or $\delta_{min} \approx (\mu - 1)A$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: In an ideal p–n junction diode, current in forward bias increases exponentially with applied voltage.
Statement II: In reverse bias, the diode current is nearly constant (saturation current) until breakdown.
questions.explanation: The Shockley diode equation is $I = I_s(e^{V/(nV_T)} - 1)$. In forward bias, when $V$ increases beyond threshold ($\sim 0.6$–$0.7$ V for Si), current rises exponentially. In reverse bias, $I \approx -I_s$, a very small saturation current due to minority carriers, until reverse breakdown occurs. Thus both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about nuclear binding energy is correct?

options.a: Binding energy per nucleon is highest for hydrogen
options.b: Iron-group nuclei have high binding energy per nucleon, so both fusion of light nuclei and fission of heavy nuclei release energy
options.c: Binding energy per nucleon increases monotonically with mass number
options.d: A nucleus with higher total binding energy is less stable

questions.explanation: The binding energy per nucleon curve peaks at $A \approx 56$ (iron/nickel) at around 8.8 MeV/nucleon. Light nuclei ($A<56$) release energy by fusion, and heavy nuclei ($A>56$) release energy by fission, as both move towards greater stability near iron. Therefore, the statement about iron-group nuclei being most stable and energy released in both fusion and fission is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Arrange the following electromagnetic waves in order of increasing frequency: microwaves (M), ultraviolet (UV), X-rays (X), infrared (IR).
questions.explanation: The electromagnetic spectrum is ordered by frequency as follows: Radio waves < Microwaves < Infrared < Visible < Ultraviolet < X-rays < Gamma rays. Thus, for the given options: microwaves (lowest) < infrared < ultraviolet < X-rays (highest).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A copper wire of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ carries current $I$. Which expression gives the drift speed $v_d$ of electrons (number density $n$, charge $e$)?
questions.explanation: Current density is $J = I/A$. Also, $J = ne v_d$, where $n$ is electron number density, $e$ is electron charge, and $v_d$ is drift velocity. Thus $v_d = I/(neA)$. The length $L$ of the wire does not affect the drift velocity directly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical capacitors (each $C$) are first connected in series and charged to $V$. They are then disconnected and reconnected in parallel (positive to positive). What is the total electrostatic energy stored after reconnection?
questions.explanation: Initially: two capacitors in series, $C_{eq} = C/2$. Stored energy $U_i = \tfrac{1}{2}(C/2)V^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}CV^2$. Each capacitor carries charge $Q = C(V/2)$. After parallel connection (positive to positive), total charge $Q_{tot} = 2Q = CV$. Effective capacitance = $2C$, so common voltage $V_f = Q_{tot}/(2C) = V/2$. Final energy $U_f = \tfrac{1}{2}(2C)(V/2)^2 = \tfrac{1}{8}CV^2$. This is half of the initial series energy. The lost energy is dissipated as heat during redistribution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an AC circuit with impedance $Z$ and power factor $\cos\phi$, the average power consumed is:
questions.explanation: In AC, instantaneous power is $p(t) = v(t)i(t)$. The time average over a cycle gives $P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$, where $\phi$ is the phase difference between voltage and current. If the circuit is purely resistive, $\cos\phi=1$ and $P=VI$. If purely reactive, $\cos\phi=0$ and $P=0$. Thus the correct formula is $P = VI\cos\phi$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capillary tube of radius $r$ is dipped vertically in a liquid of surface tension $S$ and contact angle $\theta$. The rise of liquid column $h$ is:
questions.explanation: When a capillary tube is dipped, surface tension pulls the liquid upward along the inner wall. The vertical component of the surface tension force is $F = 2\pi r S \cos\theta$. The weight of the liquid column of height $h$ is $W = \pi r^2 h \rho g$. Equating forces: $2\pi r S \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g$. Simplifying gives $h = \tfrac{2S \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$. The rise is inversely proportional to tube radius, directly proportional to $S$, and depends on wetting angle $\theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: The total mechanical energy of a satellite in circular orbit of radius $r$ is $E = -\tfrac{GMm}{2r}$.
Statement II: Orbital speed decreases with increasing $r$ as $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$.

options.a: Both statements are true and II supports I
options.b: Both statements are true but II does not support I

questions.explanation: For a satellite in circular orbit: gravitational potential energy $U = -\tfrac{GMm}{r}$, kinetic energy $K = +\tfrac{GMm}{2r}$, so total $E = K+U = -\tfrac{GMm}{2r}$. Orbital speed $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$ decreases with larger $r$. Both statements are correct, and the fact that $E$ depends inversely on $r$ is consistent with the expression for $v$, so II supports I.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In photoelectric effect, stopping potential depends on light frequency but not intensity.
Reason (R): Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons is $K_{max} = h\nu - \phi$, independent of intensity.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: From Einstein’s photoelectric equation: $K_{max} = h\nu - \phi = eV_s$. Stopping potential $V_s$ depends only on $\nu$ (light frequency) and work function $\phi$, but not on intensity. Intensity changes the number of emitted electrons (hence current), but not their maximum energy. Thus both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which mode of heat transfer can occur through a vacuum between two bodies at different temperatures?
questions.explanation: Conduction requires particle collisions and convection requires a fluid medium. In a vacuum, no matter is present, so neither conduction nor convection can occur. Heat transfer is possible only by radiation, which involves electromagnetic waves and does not need a medium. This is why the Sun’s heat reaches Earth through space.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
A simple pendulum has period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}$. Which statements are correct?
1) $T$ is independent of bob mass.
2) $T$ is independent of amplitude (small oscillations).
3) $T$ decreases if $g$ decreases.
questions.explanation:
From $T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}$:
1) Mass $m$ does not appear, so $T$ is independent of mass.
2) For small angles (small oscillations), approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ holds, making $T$ independent of amplitude.
3) If $g$ decreases, denominator decreases, so $T$ increases (not decreases). Hence only statements 1 and 2 are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic field inside a long, tightly wound solenoid with $n$ turns per unit length carrying current $I$ is:
questions.explanation: For a solenoid, apply Ampere’s circuital law along a rectangular path through the solenoid: $\oint B\,dl = \mu_0 I_{enc}$. If there are $n$ turns per unit length, total current enclosed is $nIl$ for length $l$. This gives $Bl = \mu_0 nIl$, or $B = \mu_0 n I$. Options (b), (c), and (d) correspond to field of circular loops or long straight wires, not solenoids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: For an ideal gas, $C_p - C_v = R$ (per mole).
Statement II: The difference arises because enthalpy $H$ includes $pV$ work whereas internal energy $U$ does not.

options.a: Both statements are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
options.b: Both statements are true but Statement II does not explain Statement I

questions.explanation: For 1 mole of an ideal gas: $H = U + pV$. Since $pV = RT$, differentiating with respect to $T$ gives $dH = dU + RdT$. By definition, $C_p = (\partial H/\partial T)_p$ and $C_v = (\partial U/\partial T)_v$, so $C_p - C_v = R$. Physically, constant-pressure heating requires extra energy to allow expansion work, included in $H$ but not in $U$. Thus both statements are true and II explains I.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A buffer solution is made by mixing $0.20$ mol CH$_3$COOH ($K_a = 1.8\times10^{−5}$) and $0.10$ mol CH$_3$COONa in enough water to make $1.0$ L. What is the pH at 25 °C?
questions.explanation: Henderson–Hasselbalch equation: $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\tfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$. $pK_a = -\log(1.8\times10^{-5}) \approx 4.74$. Ratio $[A^-]/[HA] = 0.10/0.20 = 0.5$, so $\log 0.5 = -0.301$. Thus $\text{pH} = 4.74 - 0.301 = 4.44$. Therefore the buffer solution has pH 4.44.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction N$_2$O$_4$(g) ⇌ 2NO$_2$(g), $\Delta H^\circ > 0$. Which set of changes will increase the equilibrium degree of dissociation of N$_2$O$_4$?
questions.explanation: The forward dissociation reaction $N_2O_4 \to 2NO_2$ produces more moles of gas and is endothermic. By Le Chatelier’s principle: decreasing pressure favors higher number of gas molecules, and increasing temperature favors the endothermic direction. Therefore, both changes increase the degree of dissociation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A first-order reaction has a half-life of $23.1$ min. How long will it take for the reactant concentration to drop to $12.5\%$ of its initial value?
questions.explanation: $12.5\% = 1/8$ of the initial concentration, which corresponds to 3 half-lives ($1/2^3 = 1/8$). Time required = $3 \times 23.1 = 69.3$ min. Hence the answer is option (c).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: For a galvanic cell operating reversibly, the maximum non-PV work obtainable equals $-\Delta G$.
Statement II: At equilibrium, cell EMF is zero because $\Delta G = 0$.

options.a: Both statements are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
options.b: Both statements are true but Statement II does not explain Statement I

questions.explanation: For a reversible galvanic cell, maximum electrical work = $-\Delta G$. Also $E_{cell} = -\Delta G/(nF)$. At equilibrium, $\Delta G=0$ so $E_{cell}=0$. Both statements are true, but Statement II (equilibrium condition) is not the reason why $w_{max}=-\Delta G$. Therefore option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A $0.100$ m solution of a nonelectrolyte in water freezes at $−0.186$ °C. What is the cryoscopic constant ($K_f$) of water implied by this data?
questions.explanation: Freezing point depression: $\Delta T_f = K_f m i$. Here $\Delta T_f = 0.186$ K, $m=0.100$, $i=1$ (nonelectrolyte). Thus $K_f = 0.186/0.100 = 1.86$ K·kg·mol$^{−1}$. Therefore, option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement is correct regarding Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) in octahedral complexes?

options.b: Low-spin $d^6$ has higher CFSE than high-spin $d^6$ in the same ligand field
options.c: CFSE is independent of pairing energy

questions.explanation: In octahedral fields: high-spin $d^6 = t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$, CFSE = $(-0.4\Delta_o)$. Low-spin $d^6 = t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$, CFSE = $-2.4\Delta_o$. Thus low-spin $d^6$ has much higher stabilization compared to high-spin $d^6$ if pairing energy is low enough. CFSE depends on pairing energy and is negative (stabilizing).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent sequence will best convert benzene to m-nitrobenzoic acid?

options.d: Nitration $\to$ Sulfonation $\to$ Side\text{-}chain alkylation $\to$ Oxidation $\to$ Desulfonation

questions.explanation: To prepare m-nitrobenzoic acid: first perform Friedel--Crafts acylation on benzene, introducing an acyl group (meta-directing). Next, nitration introduces $NO_2$ group at the meta position relative to the acyl group. Finally, oxidize the acyl side chain (${-}COCH_3$) to carboxyl (${-}COOH$), giving m-nitrobenzoic acid. Other sequences fail because nitration first would deactivate the ring too much for Friedel--Crafts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which pair is correctly matched for qualitative analysis of cations?

options.a: Group II: H$_2$S in alkaline medium → sulfides of Cu$^{2+}$, Pb$^{2+}$
options.b: Group I: Dilute HCl → chlorides of Ag$^+$, Pb$^{2+}$, Hg$_2^{2+}$
options.c: Group III: NH$_4$OH in presence of NH$_4$Cl → hydroxides of Ca$^{2+}$, Sr$^{2+}$, Ba$^{2+}$
options.d: Group IV: (NH$_4$)$_2$CO$_3$ in NH$_4$Cl → carbonates of Fe$^{3+}$, Al$^{3+}$

questions.explanation: In qualitative inorganic analysis: Group I cations (Ag$^+$, Pb$^{2+}$, Hg$_2^{2+}$) are precipitated as chlorides with dilute HCl. Group II uses H$_2$S in acidic medium (not alkaline). Group III forms hydroxides of Fe$^{3+}$, Al$^{3+}$, Cr$^{3+}$. Group IV forms carbonates of Ca$^{2+}$, Sr$^{2+}$, Ba$^{2+}$. Hence option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
In the electrolytic refining of copper, which statements are correct?
1) Impure copper acts as anode.
2) Pure copper is deposited at cathode.
3) Anode mud may contain Ag and Au.
4) Electrolyte is acidified CuSO$_4$ solution.
questions.explanation: In copper electrorefining: the impure Cu slab is the anode, pure Cu sheets are cathodes, and electrolyte is CuSO$_4$ with a little H$_2$SO$_4$ to increase conductivity. At the anode, Cu dissolves as Cu$^{2+}$ ions; at the cathode, Cu$^{2+}$ deposits as pure copper. Less reactive noble metals (Ag, Au, Pt) do not dissolve and remain as anode mud. Therefore, all four statements are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Statement I: For a galvanic cell at fixed composition, the sign of the temperature coefficient of EMF depends on $\Delta S^\circ$, and the EMF decreases with temperature when $\Delta S^\circ < 0$.
Statement II: At constant pressure, $\left(\tfrac{\partial E^\circ}{\partial T}\right)_p = \tfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{nF}$.

options.a: Both statements are true and Statement II correctly explains Statement I
options.b: Both statements are true but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
options.d: Statement I is false but Statement II is true

questions.explanation: From electrochemical thermodynamics: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ and $\left(\tfrac{\partial \Delta G^\circ}{\partial T}\right)_p = -\Delta S^\circ$. Differentiating $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ with respect to $T$ at constant pressure gives $\left(\tfrac{\partial E^\circ}{\partial T}\right)_p = \tfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{nF}$. Therefore, if $\Delta S^\circ < 0$, then $\left(\tfrac{\partial E^\circ}{\partial T}\right)_p < 0$ and $E^\circ$ decreases with $T$; if $\Delta S^\circ > 0$, $E^\circ$ increases with $T$. Thus both statements are true and II explains I.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A buffer is prepared by mixing $0.20$ mol NH$_3$ ($K_b = 1.8\times10^{−5}$) and $0.20$ mol NH$_4$Cl in $1.00$ L solution at 25 °C. What is the pH?
questions.explanation: For a basic buffer (base/conjugate acid): $\text{pOH} = pK_b + \log\tfrac{[\text{salt}]}{[\text{base}]}$. Here $pK_b = -\log(1.8\times10^{-5}) = 4.74$. With equimolar NH$_3$ and NH$_4^+$, the ratio is 1 so $\log 1 = 0$, giving $\text{pOH} = 4.74$. Therefore $\text{pH} = 14.00 - 4.74 = 9.26$ at 25 °C. (Using $pK_a + pK_b = 14$ would give the same result.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is the most acidic?
questions.explanation: The $\mathrm{NO_2}$ group is strongly $-I$ and $-M$ (electron-withdrawing), stabilizing the phenoxide anion. Para substitution maximizes resonance interaction between the ring and $\mathrm{NO_2}$, enhancing anion stabilization. In o-nitrophenol, intramolecular H-bonding reduces solvation of the anion, slightly lowering acidity compared with the para isomer. Meta position lacks resonance interaction (only $-I$), so it is less acidic than o/p. Order: phenol < m-nitrophenol < o-nitrophenol < p-nitrophenol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A nonelectrolyte (10.0 g) is dissolved in 250 g water. The boiling point elevation is 0.640 K ($K_b$ for water = 0.512 K·kg·mol$^{−1}$). What is the molar mass of the solute?
questions.explanation: Elevation: $\Delta T_b = K_b m$ (since $i = 1$ for a nonelectrolyte). Thus $m = \Delta T_b/K_b = 0.640/0.512 = 1.25\,\text{mol·kg}^{-1}$. Moles solute $= m \times$ mass of solvent (kg) $= 1.25 \times 0.250 = 0.3125$ mol. Molar mass $= \dfrac{10.0\,\text{g}}{0.3125\,\text{mol}} = 32\,\text{g·mol}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A high-spin octahedral complex shows a spin-only magnetic moment of 4.90 BM. Which metal ion (in aqueous complex) most likely exhibits this value?
questions.explanation: Spin-only moment: $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ BM. Setting $\mu \approx 4.90$ gives $n(n+2) \approx 24 \Rightarrow n = 4$ unpaired electrons. High-spin Fe$^{2+}$ (d$^6$) in octahedral $\to t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$ with 4 unpaired electrons (matches 4.90 BM). Mn$^{2+}$ (d$^5$) gives $\approx 5.92$ BM, Co$^{2+}$ (d$^7$) $\approx 3.87$ BM, Ni$^{2+}$ (d$^8$) $\approx 2.83$ BM.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Arrange the following oxides in order of increasing acidity: Na$_2$O, MgO, Al$_2$O$_3$, SiO$_2$.
questions.explanation: Across Period 3 (Na→Si), metallic/basic character decreases, covalent/acidic character increases. Thus basic oxides Na$_2$O < MgO < amphoteric Al$_2$O$_3$ < acidic SiO$_2$. Therefore increasing acidity is Na$_2$O < MgO < Al$_2$O$_3$ < SiO$_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): For a first-order reaction, the half-life is independent of initial concentration.
Reason (R): For first-order kinetics, $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, $\ln\tfrac{[A]_0}{[A]} = kt$. Setting $[A] = [A]_0/2$ gives $\ln 2 = kt_{1/2} \Rightarrow t_{1/2} = (\ln 2)/k = 0.693/k$. Since $t_{1/2}$ depends only on $k$, not on $[A]_0$, the half-life is independent of initial concentration. Hence R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent most cleanly converts a primary alcohol R–CH$_2$OH to the corresponding alkyl chloride R–CH$_2$Cl with minimal side reactions?
questions.explanation: SOCl$_2$/pyridine converts alcohols to alkyl chlorides under mild conditions with gaseous byproducts (SO$_2$, HCl), simplifying purification and minimizing rearrangements. Lucas reagent (HCl/ZnCl$_2$) is best for secondary/tertiary alcohols (SN1). PCl$_5$ is harsher and can cause side reactions; NaCl/H$_2$SO$_4$ is not an efficient general method.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A metal crystallizes in a face-centered cubic (fcc) lattice with edge length $a = 400$ pm. What is the metallic radius (in pm)?
questions.explanation: In fcc, nearest neighbors touch along a face diagonal, so $4r = \sqrt{2}\,a$. Hence $r = (\sqrt{2}/4)a = a/(2\sqrt{2})$. With $a=400$ pm: $r = 400/(2\times1.414) \approx 400/2.828 \approx 141$ pm.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following polymers is obtained by condensation polymerization?
questions.explanation: Nylon-6,6 is formed by condensation of adipic acid (HOOC–(CH$_2$)$_4$–COOH) with hexamethylenediamine (H$_2$N–(CH$_2$)$_6$–NH$_2$), eliminating small molecules (H$_2$O) and creating amide linkages. Polythene, polypropylene, and PVC arise from chain-growth (addition) polymerization of alkenes/vinyl monomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The rate of a reaction generally increases with increase in temperature.
Reason (R): An increase in temperature increases the fraction of molecules having energy greater than the activation energy.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Arrhenius equation: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$. As $T$ increases, the exponential term increases because $-E_a/(RT)$ becomes less negative, so $k$ (and thus rate) increases. Kinetic theory/Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution shows that a higher $T$ increases the fraction of molecules with $E \ge E_a$. Hence both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Quantitatively, a common empirical rule is that rates roughly double for a $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise when $E_a$ is moderate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which gas law combination leads to the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$?

options.a: Boyle’s law + Avogadro’s law + Dalton’s law
options.b: Boyle’s law + Charles’s law + Avogadro’s law
options.c: Charles’s law + Graham’s law + Avogadro’s law
options.d: Boyle’s law + Graham’s law + Dalton’s law

questions.explanation: Boyle: $P \propto 1/V\,(T,n\,\text{const})$; Charles: $V \propto T\,(P,n\,\text{const})$; Avogadro: $V \propto n\,(P,T\,\text{const})$. Combining gives $V \propto \tfrac{nT}{P}$ or $PV \propto nT$. Introducing the proportionality constant $R$ yields $PV = nRT$. (Dalton’s and Graham’s laws treat mixtures and diffusion, not the single-gas state equation.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 10 days. What fraction of the original sample remains after 30 days?
questions.explanation: Number of half-lives $= 30/10 = 3$. Remaining fraction $= \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \tfrac{1}{8} = 12.5\%$. The activity and amount both decay exponentially as $N=N_0 e^{-kt}$ with $t_{1/2}=\tfrac{\ln 2}{k}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of oxidizing power of halogens in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation: Oxidizing strength correlates with standard reduction potentials $E^\circ(X_2/X^-)$. Values (aq): $E^\circ$(F$_2$/F$^-$)$\approx +2.87$ V $>$ Cl$_2$/$\text{Cl}^-\,(+1.36\,\text{V}) >$ Br$_2$/$\text{Br}^-\,(+1.09\,\text{V}) >$ I$_2$/$\text{I}^-\,(+0.54\,\text{V})$. Higher $E^\circ$ means a stronger tendency to be reduced (i.e., stronger oxidizing agent). Thus $\mathrm{F_2 > Cl_2 > Br_2 > I_2}$. (In water, F$_2$ is so strong it can oxidize H$_2$O.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A metal has a body-centered cubic (bcc) structure with edge length $a$. What is the relationship between atomic radius ($r$) and edge length?
questions.explanation: In bcc, nearest neighbors touch along the body diagonal. Body diagonal length $= \sqrt{3}\,a$, which equals $4r$ (two radii from a corner atom to the center atom and two radii to the opposite corner atom). Hence $\sqrt{3}a = 4r \Rightarrow r = (\sqrt{3}a)/4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Arrange the following in order of increasing basicity in aqueous medium: Aniline, p-Toluidine, p-Nitroaniline.
questions.explanation: Basicity of aryl amines depends on the electron density on N. $p$-Nitroaniline has a strongly electron-withdrawing $\mathrm{-NO_2}$ ($-I$, $-M$), greatly decreasing lone-pair availability $\Rightarrow$ least basic. Aniline is intermediate. $p$-Toluidine has an electron-donating $\mathrm{-CH_3}$ ($+I$) that slightly increases electron density, enhancing basicity. Thus: p-Nitroaniline < Aniline < p-Toluidine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In the electrolytic cell for sodium extraction, molten NaCl is electrolyzed. Which statement is correct?

options.a: At cathode, Na$^+$ ions are reduced to Na metal
options.b: At anode, Na$^+$ is oxidized to Na
options.c: At cathode, Cl$^−$ is oxidized to Cl$_2$

questions.explanation: Downs process (molten NaCl): Cathode (reduction): $\mathrm{Na^+ + e^- \to Na}$. Anode (oxidation): $\mathrm{2Cl^- \to Cl_2 + 2e^-}$. Hence Na metal forms at the cathode; chlorine gas evolves at the anode. (In aqueous solution this does not work due to water reduction prior to Na$^+$.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent sequence will best achieve the conversion: benzene → p-bromoaniline?
questions.explanation: Direct aniline formation first is problematic (strongly activating $-\mathrm{NH_2}$ gives polysubstitution). Sequence (b): brominate benzene to bromobenzene (Br is o/p-directing, deactivating), then nitrate to obtain mainly o-/p-bromonitrobenzene (para favored sterically), then reduce $\mathrm{NO_2}$ to $\mathrm{NH_2}$ → p-bromoaniline. (For higher para selectivity in practice, acetanilide protection or other strategies are used, but among given options (b) is best.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which type of hybridization and geometry is exhibited by [Ni(CN)$_4$]$^{2−}$?
questions.explanation: Ni$^{2+}$ is 3d$^8$. With strong-field CN$^-$ ligands, electrons in 3d pair to allow a low-spin configuration. The complex uses $\mathrm{dsp^2}$ hybrid orbitals (one $d$, one $s$, two $p$) to form four coplanar bonds → square planar geometry. This contrasts with weak-field ligands (e.g., $\mathrm{Cl^-}$) where tetrahedral sp$^3$ may be favored for d$^8$ systems.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which drug is commonly used as both an antipyretic and an analgesic?
questions.explanation: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) effectively reduces fever (antipyretic) and relieves mild to moderate pain (analgesic) via central inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis. Ibuprofen is also antipyretic/analgesic but is primarily classified as an NSAID with notable anti-inflammatory action; ranitidine is an H$_2$ receptor antagonist (acid reducer); diazepam is an anxiolytic/benzodiazepine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Bakelite is obtained by the condensation polymerization of:
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting polymer formed by condensation of phenol and formaldehyde under acidic or basic conditions. Initially, linear chains form (novolac or resol), which then crosslink upon heating to give a rigid 3D structure. Options (a), (b), (d) correspond to Teflon, Nylon-6, and Nylon-6,6 respectively, not Bakelite.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement is true about the Lanthanide contraction?

options.a: It is the gradual increase in atomic radius from La to Lu
options.b: It causes Zr and Hf to have nearly identical atomic radii
options.c: It decreases ionization enthalpy regularly across the series
options.d: It arises due to poor shielding by 4f electrons, leading to increased effective nuclear charge

questions.explanation: Lanthanide contraction is the gradual decrease (not increase) in atomic and ionic radii from La to Lu, caused by poor shielding of nuclear charge by 4f electrons. A consequence is that 4d and 5d elements (e.g., Zr and Hf) end up with almost identical radii. Thus option (b) correctly states an important result of lanthanide contraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following arrangements shows the correct order of increasing first ionization enthalpy (Period 3 elements)?
questions.explanation: Across a period, ionization enthalpy generally increases due to higher nuclear charge, but Al has lower ionization enthalpy than Mg because the electron removed from Al is a 3p electron (higher energy, more shielded) compared to Mg’s 3s electron. Therefore, the correct order is Na < Al < Mg < Si.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement about the entropy change ($\Delta S$) in phase transitions at equilibrium is correct?
questions.explanation: At equilibrium for a phase transition, $\Delta G = 0$. Since $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, rearranging gives $\Delta S = \Delta H/T$. For melting/vaporization, $\Delta H > 0$ so $\Delta S > 0$; for freezing/condensation, $\Delta H < 0$ so $\Delta S < 0$. Thus the general expression is $\Delta S = \Delta H/T$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent would best distinguish between aldehydes and ketones?
questions.explanation: Both Tollens’ reagent and Fehling’s solution are positive for aldehydes but not for most ketones. Tollens’ test gives a silver mirror as aldehydes reduce Ag$^+$ to metallic Ag. Fehling’s solution gives a brick-red Cu$_2$O precipitate when aldehydes reduce Cu$^{2+}$. Ketones generally do not react with these, except alpha-hydroxy ketones. 2,4-DNP reacts with both aldehydes and ketones, so it cannot distinguish them.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing order of thermal stability: Li$_2$CO$_3$, Na$_2$CO$_3$, K$_2$CO$_3$.
questions.explanation: Thermal stability of carbonates increases down Group 1 due to decreasing polarization of the carbonate ion by the larger cations. Li$^+$, being very small, strongly polarizes CO$_3^{2−}$ and destabilizes it, so Li$_2$CO$_3$ decomposes most easily. Na$_2$CO$_3$ is more stable, and K$_2$CO$_3$ is the most stable. Hence order: Li$_2$CO$_3$ < Na$_2$CO$_3$ < K$_2$CO$_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Graphite is a good conductor of electricity, while diamond is an insulator.
Reason (R): In graphite, each carbon atom is sp$^2$ hybridized with delocalized $\pi$ electrons, while in diamond, each carbon is sp$^3$ hybridized with localized bonds.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Graphite consists of layers of sp$^2$ hybridized carbon atoms with delocalized $\pi$ electrons that move freely within the layers, making it conductive. Diamond has each carbon sp$^3$ hybridized in a rigid tetrahedral lattice with localized electrons, so it cannot conduct electricity. Thus both statements are true and R explains A.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Which of the following solutions will have the highest boiling point elevation?
(i) 0.1 m glucose, (ii) 0.1 m NaCl, (iii) 0.1 m BaCl$_2$, (iv) 0.1 m Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$
questions.explanation: Boiling point elevation: $\Delta T_b = i K_b m$. Here $m=0.1$ for all. Van’t Hoff factor $i$ values: glucose = 1 (nonelectrolyte), NaCl ≈ 2, BaCl$_2$ ≈ 3, Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$ ≈ 5. The highest $i$ gives the greatest colligative effect. Therefore, Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$ solution has the maximum boiling point elevation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement about coordination compounds is correct?

options.a: In [Co(NH$_3$)$_6$]Cl$_3$, the Cl atoms are outside the coordination sphere

questions.explanation: In [Co(NH$_3$)$_6$]Cl$_3$, six NH$_3$ ligands coordinate to Co$^{3+}$, forming the coordination sphere. The three Cl$^−$ ions are counter-ions outside the sphere. In [Ag(NH$_3$)$_2$]+, the coordination number is 2 (not 6). In [Fe(CN)$_6$]$^{4−}$, Fe has coordination number 6. In [Cu(NH$_3$)$_4$]$^{2+}$, Cu is in +2 state (not +1).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about ozone depletion?

options.a: CFCs release chlorine atoms that catalytically decompose ozone
options.b: CO$_2$ is the main gas causing ozone depletion
options.d: NOx gases have no role in ozone depletion

questions.explanation: CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) release chlorine radicals in the stratosphere upon UV photolysis. These radicals catalytically destroy ozone: $\mathrm{Cl· + O_3 → ClO· + O_2}$; $\mathrm{ClO· + O → Cl· + O_2}$. CO$_2$ causes global warming but is not an ozone-depleting gas. NOx also contributes to ozone destruction. Hence option (a) is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Assertion (A): The rate constant of a reaction increases exponentially with increase in temperature.
Reason (R): According to the Arrhenius equation, $k = A e^{−E_a/RT}$, a small increase in T decreases the exponential factor significantly because $E_a/RT$ decreases.

options.a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Arrhenius behavior says $k = Ae^{-E_a/(RT)}$. As $T$ rises, the ratio $E_a/RT$ becomes smaller, so the negative exponent becomes less negative; the exponential term increases rapidly and hence $k$ increases sharply (often approximated by the rule of thumb that rates roughly double for a 10 °C rise when $E_a$ is moderate). Taking natural logs, $\ln k = \ln A - E_a/(RT)$, the linear $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ plot further shows why even small changes in $1/T$ can significantly change $k$. Therefore both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following salts will show highest pH in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation: Na$_2$CO$_3$ is derived from a strong base (NaOH) and a diprotic weak acid (H$_2$CO$_3$). In water, CO$_3^{2-}$ undergoes basic hydrolysis: $\mathrm{CO_3^{2-} + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HCO_3^- + OH^-}$, producing the largest $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$ among the options and thus the highest pH. CH$_3$COONa (strong base + weak acid) is also basic but weaker because $\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$ is a weaker base than $\mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}$. NaCl (strong acid + strong base) is neutral (pH ≈ 7). NH$_4$Cl (weak base + strong acid) is acidic (NH$_4^+$ hydrolyzes to produce H$_3$O$^+$). Hence Na$_2$CO$_3$ gives the highest pH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following represents a disproportionation reaction?
questions.explanation: In a disproportionation, the same element is simultaneously oxidized and reduced. In $\mathrm{2H_2O_2 \to 2H_2O + O_2}$, oxygen starts at oxidation state −1 in H$_2$O$_2$; it is reduced to −2 in H$_2$O and oxidized to 0 in O$_2$ at the same time. The other reactions are straightforward redox (combination or single displacement) without the same element undergoing both oxidation and reduction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent converts nitrobenzene to aniline?
questions.explanation: Acidic metal reductions (Sn/HCl, Fe/HCl, Zn/HCl) all convert nitrobenzene to aniline via stepwise reduction of $\mathrm{-NO_2}$ to $\mathrm{-NH_2}$. A typical sequence (with Sn/HCl) is: nitrobenzene $\xrightarrow[\text{HCl}]{\text{Sn}}$ anilinium chloride (Ph–NH$_3^+$Cl$^-$), which upon basification yields aniline (Ph–NH$_2$). Fe/HCl and Zn/HCl operate analogously; catalytic hydrogenation (H$_2$/Pd) is another viable route.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement is correct about the comparison of CO and CO$_2$ molecules?

options.a: CO$_2$ is polar, CO is nonpolar
options.b: Bond order of CO is greater than that of CO$_2$
options.c: Both CO and CO$_2$ are linear molecules
options.d: CO$_2$ has stronger bonding than CO

questions.explanation: Both CO (diatomic) and CO$_2$ (O=C=O) are linear. Option (a) is false because CO$_2$ is nonpolar overall (linear symmetry cancels dipoles), whereas CO has a small dipole moment. Option (b) is potentially true if one compares the CO bond order (≈3) with each individual C=O bond order in CO$_2$ (2), but phrasing "bond order of CO$_2$" is ambiguous for a polyatomic molecule; the unambiguous, always-correct statement is that both molecules are linear. Option (d) is false—CO has an exceptionally strong bond (very high bond dissociation energy), stronger than a single C=O in CO$_2$.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): In C4 plants, the first stable product of carbon fixation is oxaloacetic acid (OAA).
Reason (R): C4 plants use phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) carboxylase in mesophyll cells to fix CO2 initially.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: C4 plants (e.g., maize, sugarcane, sorghum) initially fix CO$_2$ in mesophyll cells by PEP carboxylase, producing oxaloacetic acid (OAA), a 4-carbon compound. This OAA is later converted into malate or aspartate, transported to bundle sheath cells, and decarboxylated to release CO$_2$ for the Calvin cycle. In contrast, C3 plants directly fix CO$_2$ by Rubisco to form 3-PGA. Hence both A and R are correct, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant hormone is responsible for promoting seed dormancy?
questions.explanation: Abscisic acid (ABA) promotes seed dormancy by suppressing germination, closing stomata under stress, and inducing storage protein synthesis. Gibberellins (GA) act antagonistically by breaking dormancy and promoting germination. Auxins regulate cell elongation and differentiation, while cytokinins promote cell division and delay senescence.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plants are commonly used for phytoremediation of heavy metal contaminated soils?
questions.explanation: Brassica juncea (Indian mustard) and Helianthus annuus (sunflower) are hyperaccumulators that absorb and store heavy metals like Pb, Cd, and As from soils. This makes them valuable for phytoremediation. Rice, wheat, pea, and chickpea are not efficient hyperaccumulators, while Cuscuta and Orobanche are parasitic plants.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about water transport in plants is correct according to the cohesion-tension theory?

options.a: Water is actively pumped upward by xylem
options.b: Transpiration pull generates a negative pressure that pulls water column upward
options.c: Root pressure is the main force lifting water to great heights
options.d: Cohesion between water and xylem walls is unimportant

questions.explanation: The cohesion-tension theory (proposed by Dixon and Joly) explains ascent of sap. Transpiration from leaves creates negative pressure (tension), which pulls the continuous column of water upward through xylem. Cohesion between water molecules (due to H-bonding) and adhesion with xylem walls maintain continuity. Root pressure can move water only short distances and is insufficient in tall trees.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Casparian strips in root endodermis are chemically composed of:
questions.explanation: Casparian strips are bands of suberin (a hydrophobic material) deposited in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. They block apoplastic transport of water and solutes, forcing them into the symplastic pathway through the plasma membrane, ensuring selective uptake of minerals.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Root nodules of leguminous plants contain nitrogen-fixing bacteria.
Reason (R): Rhizobium bacteria convert atmospheric N2 into ammonia using nitrogenase enzyme under anaerobic conditions provided by leghaemoglobin.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Leguminous plants harbor Rhizobium in root nodules. The bacteria fix atmospheric N$_2$ to NH$_3$ using nitrogenase, an O$_2$-sensitive enzyme. Leghaemoglobin binds free O$_2$, maintaining low O$_2$ concentration while providing enough for respiration. This ensures efficient nitrogen fixation. Hence both A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which genetic technique involves transfer of genes using Agrobacterium tumefaciens?
questions.explanation: Agrobacterium tumefaciens carries the Ti (tumor-inducing) plasmid. Its T-DNA integrates into the plant genome, a natural gene transfer system harnessed in genetic engineering. Biolistics uses gene guns, microinjection uses needles, and electroporation uses electrical pulses to make membranes permeable.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: In CAM plants, stomata open:
questions.explanation: CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) plants open stomata at night to minimize water loss. They fix CO$_2$ into malate, stored in vacuoles. During the day, stomata remain closed and CO$_2$ is released internally from malate for photosynthesis. This adaptation helps plants survive in arid conditions.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Statement I: In monocot roots, xylem and phloem are radially arranged.
Statement II: The vascular bundles in monocot roots are polyarch (many xylem poles).
questions.explanation: Monocot roots exhibit radial vascular bundles with xylem and phloem arranged alternately in a ring around the center. They typically have more than six xylem poles (polyarch). Dicots usually show tetrarch condition with fewer xylem poles. Thus both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about double fertilization in angiosperms is correct?

options.a: One male gamete fuses with the egg to form diploid zygote, the other with two polar nuclei to form triploid endosperm
options.b: Both male gametes fuse with eggs to form twin zygotes
options.c: One male gamete degenerates, the other fertilizes egg
options.d: Male gamete fuses with antipodals to form embryo sac

questions.explanation: Double fertilization is a hallmark of angiosperms. One sperm cell fuses with the egg cell (syngamy) to form diploid zygote. The other sperm fuses with two polar nuclei in the central cell (triple fusion) to form triploid primary endosperm nucleus, which develops into endosperm. This ensures simultaneous formation of embryo and nutritive tissue.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Guard cells regulate the opening and closing of stomata.
Reason (R): Stomatal aperture depends on the turgor pressure of guard cells, which is influenced by K+ ion transport and osmotic changes.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Guard cells control stomatal aperture by changing their turgor. When K$^+$ ions actively enter guard cells, water follows osmotically, increasing turgor pressure and causing guard cells to bow outward, opening the pore. When K$^+$ efflux occurs, water leaves, turgor decreases, and the stomata close. This mechanism regulates transpiration and gas exchange. Hence both A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Golden rice was genetically engineered to be rich in which nutrient?
questions.explanation: Golden rice was developed by introducing genes from daffodil (psy) and a bacterium (crtI) into rice endosperm to enable biosynthesis of β-carotene, the precursor of vitamin A. This biofortification addresses vitamin A deficiency, especially in developing countries.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which is the correct sequence of events during germination of a seed?

options.a: Radicle emerges → Plumule emerges → Cotyledons expand
options.b: Plumule emerges → Radicle emerges → Cotyledons expand
options.c: Cotyledons expand → Radicle emerges → Plumule emerges
options.d: Radicle emerges → Cotyledons expand → Plumule emerges

questions.explanation: Seed germination begins with imbibition, followed by resumption of metabolic activity. The radicle (embryonic root) emerges first to absorb water and anchor the plant. Next, the plumule (embryonic shoot) emerges upward. In epigeal germination, cotyledons expand and photosynthesize; in hypogeal germination, they remain below. Thus the correct sequence is radicle → plumule → cotyledons.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which photoperiodic plants require exposure to short days for flowering?
questions.explanation: Short-day plants like Xanthium and soybean require night length longer than a critical photoperiod to flower. Wheat, barley, pea, and oats are long-day plants that flower when days are longer. Spinach and sugar beet are also long-day biennials. Thus option (c) is correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant hormone is widely used in inducing parthenocarpy (seedless fruits)?
questions.explanation: Parthenocarpy is the development of fruit without fertilization. Gibberellins (especially GA$_3$) are commonly applied to induce parthenocarpic fruits like grapes and apples. Auxins can also induce seedless fruits (e.g., tomatoes), but gibberellins are more widely used commercially. Cytokinins promote cell division, while ABA induces dormancy.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plant hormone is primarily responsible for the ripening of fruits like banana and tomato?
questions.explanation: Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone that triggers and accelerates fruit ripening. It induces enzymatic changes such as conversion of starch to sugars, cell wall softening, and pigment changes (chlorophyll breakdown and carotenoid synthesis). Auxins regulate growth, gibberellins promote stem elongation, and cytokinins promote cell division, but ethylene is the ripening hormone.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which enzyme is deficient in photorespiratory mutants leading to accumulation of glycolate?
questions.explanation: In photorespiration, Rubisco oxygenates RuBP, producing glycolate. Glycolate is transported to peroxisomes, where glycolate oxidase converts it to glyoxylate. Mutants deficient in glycolate oxidase cannot metabolize glycolate, leading to its accumulation and photorespiratory stress. PEP carboxylase and malate dehydrogenase are enzymes of C$_4$ metabolism.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which plants use pneumatophores for gaseous exchange?
questions.explanation: Mangrove species (e.g., Avicennia, Sonneratia) grow in swampy soils deficient in oxygen. They develop specialized aerial roots called pneumatophores, which protrude above the soil and possess lenticels for gas exchange. Desert cacti use stem photosynthesis, orchids have aerial roots with velamen, and Hydrilla performs gas exchange through submerged leaves.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Statement I: Bryophytes are called amphibians of the plant kingdom.
Statement II: They require water for fertilization because male gametes are motile.
questions.explanation: Bryophytes (mosses, liverworts) are terrestrial but depend on water for fertilization, as their male gametes (antherozoids) swim to the archegonia. This dual dependency on land and water is why they are called amphibians of the plant kingdom. Thus both statements are true.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of dicot leaves but not monocot leaves?
questions.explanation: Dicot leaves show reticulate venation, where veins form a network. Monocot leaves typically show parallel venation. Bulliform cells (for leaf rolling) and isobilateral anatomy (similar upper and lower surfaces) are monocot features. Thus reticulate venation is a distinguishing feature of dicots.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Mycorrhizal association improves mineral absorption in plants.
Reason (R): Fungal hyphae increase the surface area of roots and help in the uptake of phosphorus and other minerals.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Mycorrhiza is a symbiotic association between plant roots and fungi. The fungal hyphae extend deep and wide into the soil, thereby greatly increasing the absorptive surface area of the root system. They enhance the uptake of phosphorus, zinc, and copper and also protect roots from pathogens. Hence both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about gymnosperms is correct?

options.b: They have ovules that remain exposed before and after fertilization

questions.explanation: Gymnosperms (e.g., Pinus, Cycas) produce seeds that are not enclosed in fruits. Their ovules remain naked on megasporophylls before and after fertilization. They possess well-developed vascular tissues (xylem and phloem), but lack flowers and fruits. Double fertilization is absent in gymnosperms and unique to angiosperms. Hence option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which is the correct chronological sequence in the life cycle of angiosperms?
questions.explanation: Microsporogenesis occurs first when microspore mother cells undergo meiosis to produce microspores (pollen grains). Then microgametogenesis takes place inside pollen grains to form male gametophytes. Pollination transfers pollen to the stigma. Finally, fertilization occurs when the male gametes fuse with egg and polar nuclei. Hence option (a) is correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about photoperiodism is correct?

options.a: It is the response of plants to changes in water availability
options.b: It is controlled by the duration of light and dark periods affecting flowering
options.c: It is the breaking of seed dormancy by light
options.d: It is the movement of chloroplasts within cells due to light

questions.explanation: Photoperiodism refers to the physiological response of plants to the relative length of day and night. It plays a key role in flowering and is mediated by phytochrome pigments. Short-day plants flower when night exceeds a critical length, long-day plants flower when nights are shorter, and day-neutral plants are unaffected. Hence option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Which of the following pairs is correctly matched?
1) Cycas – Coralloid roots
2) Opuntia – Phylloclade
3) Potato – Corm
4) Onion – Bulb
questions.explanation: Cycas forms coralloid roots containing cyanobacteria for nitrogen fixation. Opuntia has flattened phylloclades for photosynthesis in arid conditions. Potato is a stem tuber, not a corm. Onion is a true bulb composed of a short stem and fleshy leaves. Hence 1, 2, and 4 are correct.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Transpiration is a necessary evil in plants.
Reason (R): Though it causes water loss, transpiration also helps in absorption and ascent of sap, and in cooling of leaves.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Transpiration leads to significant water loss, which may be harmful, hence called a 'necessary evil'. However, it is essential for creating transpiration pull, which aids ascent of sap, facilitates mineral transport, and provides cooling to leaves. Hence both A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about apical dominance is correct?

options.a: It is caused by high concentration of cytokinins in apical bud
options.b: It is maintained by auxins produced in apical bud suppressing lateral buds
options.c: It is maintained by abscisic acid accumulation in lateral buds
options.d: It is promoted by gibberellins in apical bud

questions.explanation: Apical dominance is the inhibition of lateral bud growth by auxins secreted from the shoot tip (apical bud). Removing the apical bud removes auxin influence, allowing lateral buds to grow. Cytokinins promote lateral bud growth, ABA induces dormancy, and gibberellins promote stem elongation, not apical dominance.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about the vascular bundles of dicot stems is correct?

options.a: They are scattered, closed, and without cambium
options.b: They are arranged in a ring, open, and have cambium between xylem and phloem
options.c: They are radial, closed, and exarch
options.d: They are conjoint, closed, and endarch

questions.explanation: Dicot stems show vascular bundles arranged in a ring. Each bundle is conjoint (xylem and phloem together), collateral, and open, meaning they contain cambium between xylem and phloem. This cambium allows secondary growth. Monocot stems, in contrast, have scattered closed bundles without cambium.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following plants shows hydrotropism?

options.b: Root apex bending towards water source
options.c: Climbing stems twining around a support

questions.explanation: Hydrotropism is directional growth in response to water. Roots exhibit positive hydrotropism by bending toward a water source. Other tropisms include phototropism (light), thigmotropism (touch in climbers), and heliotropism (sun tracking in sunflower).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which component of chloroplast is responsible for capturing solar energy?
questions.explanation: Grana are stacks of thylakoids, and their membranes contain chlorophyll pigments and photosystems I and II. These structures absorb light energy and initiate the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis. The stroma hosts the Calvin cycle, plastoglobuli store lipids, and the envelope membranes provide compartmentalization.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Companion cells in angiosperms are essential for the functioning of sieve tube elements.
Reason (R): Companion cells assist in loading and unloading of sucrose into sieve tubes through active transport mechanisms.

options.a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Sieve tube elements lose nuclei at maturity, becoming metabolically dependent on companion cells. Companion cells, derived from the same mother cell, retain dense cytoplasm and nuclei. They perform metabolic activities and actively transport sucrose (via proton-sucrose symporters) into and out of sieve tubes. This makes phloem loading and unloading possible. Thus both A and R are true, and R explains A.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which of the following is an adaptation of xerophytes?

options.c: Thick cuticle, sunken stomata, reduced leaves as spines

questions.explanation: Xerophytes are adapted to dry habitats. To minimize transpiration, they show features such as thick waxy cuticle, sunken stomata to trap moist air, and reduction of leaves into spines (as in cactus). Photosynthesis shifts to the stem (phylloclade). Succulent tissues also store water. Thin leaves and large intercellular spaces are typical of hydrophytes, not xerophytes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which type of placentation is seen in sunflower?
questions.explanation: In sunflower (family Asteraceae), the ovary is unilocular and has a single ovule attached at the base, which is the defining feature of basal placentation. Marginal placentation occurs in legumes (pea), axile in Hibiscus and tomato (ovules at axis), and parietal in mustard (ovules on inner ovary wall).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question:
Statement I: Phytochrome is a photoreceptor pigment sensitive to red and far-red light.
Statement II: It exists in two interconvertible forms, Pr and Pfr, regulating photoperiodic responses.
questions.explanation: Phytochrome is a blue-green pigment that senses red light (660 nm) and far-red light (730 nm). It exists in two forms: Pr (inactive, absorbs red) and Pfr (active, absorbs far-red). These two forms interconvert depending on light quality and regulate seed germination, flowering (photoperiodism), and shade avoidance. Hence both statements are true.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.question: Which statement about algae is correct?

options.a: All algae are multicellular and terrestrial
options.b: Brown algae store food as laminarin and mannitol
options.c: Red algae have chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b

questions.explanation: Brown algae (Phaeophyceae, e.g., Laminaria, Sargassum) store reserve food as laminarin (a polysaccharide) and mannitol (a sugar alcohol). Red algae (Rhodophyceae) possess chlorophyll a and d, not b. Green algae (Chlorophyceae) contain chlorophyll a and b. Many algae are unicellular and aquatic, not all are multicellular or terrestrial.
